title: Signal + Judgment Trader
subtitle: An algorithmic trading system whose key finding is an honest negative result
description:
An algorithmic trading system for liquid US equities built as a three-layer decision pipeline — ML signal model, LLM judgment filter, hard-coded risk gate — with an event-driven backtester as the "truth machine" built first. The headline result is the one most trading projects hide:

| Metric | Gate | In-sample (lookahead — invalid) | **Walk-forward out-of-sample** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sharpe | ≥ 1.0 | 1.18 | **−0.02** |
| Max drawdown | ≤ 15% | 3.1% | **16.0%** |
| Profit factor | ≥ 1.3 | 1.70 | **0.99** |
| Net return | — | +14.6% | **−1.2%** |

The same model that looks excellent scored over its own training window has **no out-of-sample edge whatsoever**. The score→return decile curve is flat-to-inverted (top-minus-bottom spread −0.03%), mean OOS AUC is 0.516 across 12 walk-forward folds with 3 folds below 0.5, and losses concentrate entirely in the 2022 bear market — the classic failure mode of long-only mean-reversion, buying dips that keep dipping.

That gap *is* the finding. The system was designed so that this outcome would be detected rather than shipped: a pre-registered backtest gate (Sharpe ≥ 1.0, max DD ≤ 15%, PF ≥ 1.3 after costs), walk-forward validation with an embargo, mutation-tested anti-lookahead guarantees, and an explicit "honest checkpoint" stating that if OOS results fail the gate, the correct outcome is to stop — not to loosen the gate or tweak the backtester. When the checkpoint fired, the gate was not loosened, the cost model was not softened, and the thesis was rejected on the evidence. The design doc listed "no real edge exists" as the most likely risk from day one; the system's willingness to stop is a feature, not a failure.

The engineering around that result is fully built and tested: a Pydantic-schema'd pipeline (signal → judgment → risk → execution) where each stage can only shrink or block what the previous stage proposes, a fail-closed Claude API judgment agent, an immutable risk layer with mandatory stops and a kill switch, deterministic reproducible backtests keyed by config hash, and paper-trading execution via Alpaca.

technicalChallenges.title: Walk-forward validation that can't lie to you
technicalChallenges.description: The in-sample row of the results table shows the trap: naive evaluation produces Sharpe 1.18 from pure lookahead. The system avoids it with a rolling walk-forward scheme — 4-year train windows, 6-month validation windows tiling history without overlap, and a 5-trading-day embargo gap between train and validation equal to the label horizon, so no 5-day forward-return label ever leaks across the boundary. At backtest time, each trading day is scored only by the fold model whose validation window contains that day, so no prediction ever comes from a model that trained on the row's own date.

technicalChallenges.title: An LLM as a fail-closed trade filter, not a trader
technicalChallenges.description: The judgment layer sends each signal plus context (earnings calendar, macro events, news) to a Claude API agent that returns APPROVE / REJECT / REDUCE_SIZE with reasoning. The contract is asymmetric by construction: Claude can only remove or shrink trades, never create, enlarge, or redirect them — and that's enforced in code (Pydantic validators clamping size multipliers to decisions, a parser that accepts only the three decision values), not just in the prompt. Any failure — API timeout, unparseable JSON, schema violation — resolves to REJECT: an unreachable judge means no trade, never a default approve.

technicalChallenges.title: Proving the absence of lookahead, not just claiming it
technicalChallenges.description: Anti-lookahead is enforced by a poisoned-future mutation test: unit fixtures corrupt all bars after date D and assert that D's computed features are bit-identical, so any accidental use of future data fails the suite immediately. Entry fills are modeled at the next day's open (matching the live fill convention), never the signal day's close, and every backtest result carries a config hash — a run that can't be reproduced exactly doesn't count.

technicalChallenges.title: A checkpoint mechanism designed to kill the project
technicalChallenges.description: The design doc pre-registered the success gate and an "honest checkpoint": if out-of-sample results don't approach the gate after reasonable iteration, stop — don't loosen the gate, don't modify the backtester, don't re-tune on the same OOS window. Diagnostics (decile calibration, score distribution, per-year PnL attribution) showed the failure was structural — no score→return relationship at all — so further feature tweaking would only have manufactured overfitting. The checkpoint fired as designed, and the negative result was written up with full reproduction steps instead of being buried.

assets.imageAlt: Signal + Judgment Trader — algorithmic trading system architecture diagram

title: Automated Content Continuity Pipeline
subtitle: Async LLM pipeline detecting continuity conflicts across versioned documents
description: Designed and built a production-grade async backend that uses LLM-powered semantic analysis to detect continuity conflicts across versioned documents. Architected a fully decoupled pipeline with FastAPI, Celery/Redis for distributed task processing, and ChromaDB for high-dimensional vector search. Implemented JWT authentication, SHA-256 document deduplication, multi-format text extraction (PDF, DOCX, Markdown), and full schema migration management with Alembic. Integrated Gemini 2.0 Flash as the semantic reasoning layer for conflict detection across document versions.

technicalChallenges.title: Non-Blocking Async Document Chunking
technicalChallenges.description: Processing large multi-format documents synchronously would block FastAPI's async event loop, causing request timeouts under concurrent load. The solution offloads all extraction and chunking work to Celery workers via Redis as the message broker, keeping the main API process entirely non-blocking. Task status is tracked via a polling endpoint backed by database state, with no long-polling or WebSocket overhead on the server side.

technicalChallenges.title: Vector Embedding Search Latency in ChromaDB
technicalChallenges.description: Naïve cosine similarity scans across large document chunk collections produced query latencies exceeding acceptable thresholds as the corpus grew. Mitigated by tuning ChromaDB's HNSW index parameters (ef_construction, M) to balance recall accuracy against query time, and pre-filtering candidate chunks by document metadata before executing the embedding distance search. This reduced P95 query latency significantly while maintaining semantic retrieval precision above 90%.

technicalChallenges.title: SHA-256 Deduplication Across Multi-Format Inputs
technicalChallenges.description: Different document formats produce structurally identical content that naive byte-hash comparison treats as distinct, causing redundant processing and inflated vector storage. A normalised text extraction layer strips format-specific encoding before hashing, so semantically identical documents across PDF, DOCX, and Markdown representations hash identically and are deduplicated at ingestion time before any LLM call is dispatched.

assets.imageAlt: Architecture diagram of the Automated Content Continuity Pipeline showing FastAPI, Celery workers, Redis broker, ChromaDB vector store, and PostgreSQL

title: Dual-Design Portfolio Website
subtitle: Two fully distinct portfolio views switchable via a persistent toggle
description: Built a production-spec portfolio platform with two entirely distinct views — a Professional Executive View and a PS Vita XMB-inspired immersive view — switchable via a single persistent toggle. Engineered a custom state management system using localStorage and custom DOM events with no framework dependency. Implemented FOUC prevention via a synchronous inline script before CSSOM construction, strict CSS namespace isolation using `.pro-` and `.vita-` class prefixes, and an audio sprite system via Howler.js. Optimised for Core Web Vitals: LCP < 1.2s, CLS 0.00.

technicalChallenges.title: FOUC Prevention via Synchronous State Lock
technicalChallenges.description: Rendering either view before the stored view preference is applied produces a flash of the wrong view on page load. The fix requires an inline synchronous script as the very first element in <head> — before any <link> or <style> — that reads localStorage and sets [data-view] on <html> before the CSSOM is constructed. Any deferral or async loading of this script reintroduces the flash, making its placement a hard architectural invariant.

technicalChallenges.title: Zero Style Leakage Between Isolated View Namespaces
technicalChallenges.description: Both views coexist in the DOM simultaneously with only CSS visibility rules controlling which is active. Without strict namespace discipline, Professional styles bleed into Vita elements and vice versa, producing visual corruption on toggle. All selectors are prefixed (.pro- or .vita-), all CSS custom properties are scoped under [data-view] attribute selectors, and @layer ordering guarantees no utility rule can silently override a view-specific component rule.

technicalChallenges.title: Sub-400ms View Transition with Cross-Browser Fallback
technicalChallenges.description: The View Transition API provides GPU-composited cross-fade transitions in Chrome 111+, but requires a fully functional manual CSS fallback for other browsers. The transition manager detects API availability at runtime and dispatches the appropriate path — either document.startViewTransition with custom ::view-transition-* keyframes, or a manually orchestrated opacity/scale animation sequence using requestAnimationFrame. Both paths must complete the full transition in under 400ms perceived time.

assets.imageAlt: Side-by-side comparison of the Professional Executive View and PS Vita Immersive View of the portfolio

title: Unity C# Algorithmic Systems
subtitle: A* pathfinding and procedural level generation in real-time C# environments
description: Developed a suite of algorithmic systems in Unity/C# focused on real-time problem solving and object-oriented design. Implemented a dynamic A* pathfinding system capable of navigating around runtime-placed obstacles, and a procedural level generation system using modular room adjacency rules to produce varied but structurally coherent level layouts. Projects were used as vehicles for deep hands-on study of data structures, algorithm complexity, and Unity's MonoBehaviour lifecycle.

technicalChallenges.title: A* Pathfinding Grid Scalability Under Dynamic Obstacles
technicalChallenges.description: A standard A* implementation on a dense grid becomes prohibitively expensive when obstacles are placed or removed at runtime, because the entire open set must be re-evaluated from the affected nodes. Addressed by implementing a hierarchical grid with region-level connectivity caching — only the affected regions invalidate their cached paths, leaving unaffected routes intact and reducing re-computation cost proportionally to the locality of the change rather than the full grid size.

technicalChallenges.title: Coherent Procedural Levels from Modular Room Adjacency
technicalChallenges.description: Purely random room placement produces disconnected or spatially overlapping layouts that are unplayable. The generation system encodes each room prefab with typed connector slots (north, south, east, west) and builds the level graph using a constraint-satisfaction pass — rooms are placed only when their connectors satisfy adjacency rules and the spatial bounding boxes do not intersect. A backtracking step resolves dead ends by inserting corridor pieces that satisfy remaining open connectors.

assets.imageAlt: Unity editor screenshot showing A* pathfinding grid overlay with obstacle placement and agent navigation path highlighted
